5: Uzziah’s leprosy — inflicted when he entered the temple to burn incense on the altar of incense, a prerogative belonging to the Aaronide priests alone (2 Chronicles 26:16-20) — is the OT’s most dramatic account of a king struck for crossing the boundary between royal and priestly offices. The Chronicler specifies the precise act: wayyābo’ ʿuzziyyāhû ʾel bêt YHWH liqṭēr ʿal mizbah haqqᵉṭōret, ‘Uzziah went into the house of YHWH to burn incense on the altar of incense’ — the initiative of a king who sought to unite in himself what the Mosaic constitution separated. The leprosy is the sign of his exclusion: he was thrust out of the sanctuary and remained leprous to his death, dwelling in a separate house while his son Jotham governed. The theological pattern is the inverse of what Hebrews 7:1-3 describes in Melchizedek and applies to Christ: aphōmoiōmenos de tō hyiō tou theou, menei hiereus eis to diēnekes — ‘resembling the Son of God, he remains a priest forever.’ Melchizedek holds both royal and priestly dignity legitimately — king of Salem, priest of God Most High — because the two offices were never separated in his person. Uzziah’s attempted unauthorized fusion (and its punishment) marks the limit of the Davidic kingship within the Mosaic constitution: no Davidic king could be priest under the Levitical law. Jesus exceeds both offices through a different order altogether — ‘not according to the law of a fleshly commandment but according to the power of an indestructible life’ (Hebrews 7:16). Jesus’s touching of lepers (Mark 1:41-42) — the one who makes the unclean clean — also reverses the Uzziah pattern: where Uzziah’s sanctuary-entry made the king leprous, Jesus’s touch makes the leprous clean.
1: Azariah’s accession synchronism opens a chapter of rapid dynastic turnover in the north. Amid the political instability — six northern kings in the compass of these verses, four by assassination — Christ stands as the contrast: “Of the increase of his government and of peace there will be no end” (Isaiah 9:7). Where Azariah’s 52-year reign is the exception in a sea of instability, Christ’s reign is the permanent eternal kingship that every human throne only approximates.
2: Azariah reigns 52 years — the longest reign in Judah’s history after Manasseh. Length of reign was a covenant blessing sign (Deuteronomy 17:20: the king who keeps the covenant will “reign long over his kingdom, he and his children, in Israel”). Christ’s reign is not merely long but eternal — “he will reign over the house of Jacob forever, and of his kingdom there will be no end” (Luke 1:33). What earthly kings achieved by years, Christ possesses by nature.
3: Azariah did right as his father Amaziah did. The partial Davidic pattern — doing right in broad terms — is the best most Judean kings achieve. Christ does not partially fulfill the covenant standard; he is “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners” (Hebrews 7:26). The high place qualification in v4 marks Azariah’s obedience as incomplete; Christ’s obedience is complete and unqualified (Hebrews 5:8-9).
4: The high places were not removed — the standard qualification that marks imperfect reform. No Judean king before Hezekiah removed the high places. This persistent worship outside Jerusalem (the divinely chosen place) typifies the problem of unauthorized approach to God. Christ removes the need for earthly sacred geography entirely: “Believe me, the hour is coming when neither on this mountain nor in Jerusalem will you worship the Father” (John 4:21). Christ is the end of the high-place problem — he himself is the meeting point.
6: The regnal summary — Azariah’s remaining acts recorded in the now-lost chronicles. The partial record of human achievement points toward the “books” that contain the full account of all deeds (Revelation 20:12). But for those in Christ, another book matters more: “The one who conquers will be clothed in white garments, and I will never blot his name out of the book of life” (Revelation 3:5). The royal chronicle records what earthly kings did; the book of life records those who are in Christ.
7: Azariah buried in the city of David — the royal burial ground that marks Davidic legitimacy. Christ is the ultimate “son of David” (Matthew 1:1) buried and raised from Jerusalem — not in a royal tomb but in a borrowed one (Matthew 27:60), and not remaining there. His resurrection transformed the meaning of royal burial: “He was not abandoned to Hades, nor did his flesh see corruption” (Acts 2:31).
8: Zechariah son of Jeroboam reigns six months — the shortest reign of any king in Kings except Zimri’s seven days. The extreme brevity of Zechariah’s reign closes the Jehu dynasty precisely at the fourth generation (Jehu → Jehoahaz → Joash → Jeroboam II → Zechariah), fulfilling the divine promise of 2 Kings 10:30. Christ ends all dynasties: “he must reign until he has put all his enemies under his feet” (1 Corinthians 15:25). Every human dynasty is temporary; his is not.
9: Zechariah did not depart from the sins of Jeroboam. The “sins of Jeroboam” formula appears repeatedly in 2 Kings 15 — it is the structural sin of the Northern Kingdom, entrenched through generations. What no Israelite king could break — the pull of Jeroboam’s calves — Christ breaks definitively: he is the true object of worship that all false substitutes distort. The sin of worshiping a wrong image is ended by the “image of the invisible God” (Colossians 1:15) who is the true icon of the Father.
10: Shallum conspires against Zechariah, strikes him down, and reigns. The cycle of violent succession in the north — assassination as the primary mode of regime change after the Jehu dynasty — is the antithesis of the Davidic covenant’s promise of dynastic continuity. Christ is the king who comes to power not through assassination but through willing self-sacrifice: “No one takes my life from me, but I lay it down of my own accord” (John 10:18).
11: The summary notice for Zechariah. The brevity of the notice reflects the brevity of the reign. Against the chaos of six-month reigns and assassinations, the promise of Psalms 2:6-7 stands: “As for me, I have set my King on Zion, my holy hill. I will tell of the decree: The LORD said to me, ‘You are my Son; today I have begotten you.’” What the northern kings could not secure by force or lineage, Christ holds by eternal decree.
12: The fulfillment of YHWH’s word to Jehu: “your sons to the fourth generation shall sit on the throne of Israel” (2 Kings 10:30) — “this is the word of the LORD that he spoke to Jehu.” The pattern of divine word → precise historical fulfillment is the grammar of redemptive history. Christ is the fullest instance of this pattern: “All the promises of God find their Yes in him” (2 Corinthians 1:20). Not one word of the LORD falls to the ground (2 Kings 10:10) — fulfilled here in Zechariah, fulfilled ultimately in Christ.
13: Shallum reigns one month and is killed by Menahem. The one-month reign is even shorter than Zechariah’s six months. The instability of earthly power — achieved by violence, lost by violence — is one of Scripture’s recurring demonstrations that human kingship requires divine grounding. Christ’s kingdom “cannot be shaken” (Hebrews 12:28) — it is established not by human conspiracy but by resurrection from the dead.
14: Menahem kills Shallum and becomes king. Each assassination creates a new would-be dynasty, none of which lasts. The perpetual instability of the northern monarchy — twelve kings in approximately two centuries, many killed by successors — is the political expression of covenant breakdown. Christ brings the new covenant whose political expression is the eternal, unassailable kingdom: “the kingdom of God is at hand” (Mark 1:15), and it does not pass from one assassin to the next.
15: Shallum’s summary — his conspiracy recorded in now-lost chronicles. The chronicle records political facts; what matters covenantally is whether the king served YHWH. Shallum’s conspiracy was politically successful for one month and then destroyed him. Christ warns against exactly this strategy: “For what does it profit a man to gain the whole world and forfeit his soul?” (Mark 8:36). The chronicles of earthly kings record gains measured in months; Christ’s kingdom measures in eternity.
16: Menahem’s brutal act against Tiphsah — ripping open pregnant women of those who would not open their gates. The atrocity against vulnerable life is one of the most disturbing acts recorded in Kings, paralleling Elisha’s Aramean vision (2 Kings 8:12). Christ is the protector of the vulnerable — “blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven” (Matthew 5:3) — and the one who calls for judgment on those who harm “one of these little ones” (Matthew 18:6). The violence of Menahem’s military power is the anti-type of Christ’s kingdom.
17: Menahem reigns ten years in Samaria — a relatively stable reign achieved by tribute payments to Assyria. The stability is purchased at the cost of vassalage: Menahem becomes a client king of Assyria. Christ refuses this kind of Satanic kingship offer: “All these I will give you, if you will fall down and worship me” — to which Jesus answers: “Worship the Lord your God and serve him only” (Matthew 4:9-10). Menahem buys his throne by bowing to Pul; Christ refuses to buy his kingdom through cosmic capitulation.
18: Menahem did evil — the sins of Jeroboam not departed from. The repeated formula in this chapter (vv9, 18, 24, 28) is not mere literary repetition but theological insistence: the northern kings are structurally embedded in Jeroboam’s apostasy and cannot escape it. Only Christ breaks the power of entrenched sin: “For sin will have no dominion over you, since you are not under law but under grace” (Romans 6:14). What the northern kings could not break free of — Jeroboam’s gravity — the gospel dismantles through union with Christ.
19: Pul (Tiglath-Pileser III) invades and Menahem pays 1,000 talents of silver for Assyrian support. The tribute relationship — paying for protection — typifies the conditional security of earthly kingdoms that rely on foreign power. Christ offers unconditional security: “Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or danger, or sword?” (Romans 8:35). No tribute required; no foreign power needed; the kingdom Christ secures cannot be purchased away.
20: The silver comes from a levy on wealthy Israelites — fifty shekels each. The taxation to fund a tribute payment to a foreign oppressor is Israel’s tribute-from-the-weak-to-pay-the-strong pattern. Christ reverses this: he pays the ultimate tribute (his own life) so that his people are set free from all such payments. “You were bought with a price; do not become slaves of men” (1 Corinthians 7:23). The fifty-shekel levy on ordinary Israelites is the type; Christ’s self-giving is the antitype that ends all debt.
21: Menahem’s regnal summary — his remaining acts in the now-lost chronicles. The record of a reign that purchased stability by subordination to Assyria is a portrait of the failure of self-protective power. Christ’s power is demonstrated in weakness: “When I am weak, then I am strong” (2 Corinthians 12:10). The strategy of Menahem (buy protection) and the strategy of Christ (give yourself) are the two poles of the power question.
22: Menahem rests and his son Pekahiah succeeds him. Dynastic succession — father to son — is the ordinary means of royal continuity. The Davidic dynasty’s stability contrasts with the northern pattern; and Christ is the final Davidic Son: “The LORD God will give him the throne of his father David, and he will reign over the house of Jacob forever” (Luke 1:32-33). The father-son succession of earthly kings is a pale reflection of the eternal Father-Son relationship within which Christ’s kingship is grounded.
23: Pekahiah’s two-year reign. The brevity again — a reign barely begun before assassination. The instability of the northern monarchy in its final decades (c. 742–722 BCE) mirrors the spiritual chaos of a people who have “forgotten the Lord’s works” (Psalms 78:11) and rejected his covenant. Christ comes as the fulfillment precisely into this chaos: “He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him” (John 1:11) — but his kingdom, unlike Pekahiah’s, cannot be cut short.
24: Pekahiah did evil — sins of Jeroboam continued. The unbroken series of “did not depart from the sins of Jeroboam” through 2 Kings 15-17 reads like a tragic chorus: no king of Israel ever broke free. But what no king could accomplish, Christ accomplishes: “Since therefore Christ suffered in the flesh, arm yourselves with the same way of thinking, for whoever has suffered in the flesh has ceased from sin” (1 Peter 4:1). The break from entrenched sin comes through death and resurrection — which is precisely what Christ provides.
25: Pekah assassinates Pekahiah with fifty Gileadite warriors. The military overthrow in the throne room — men who served become killers of their master — inverts the royal protection that kings are supposed to provide. Christ’s betrayal follows a similar pattern: “He came to his own.” But where Pekahiah dies as a powerless victim, Christ dies as the willing shepherd: “The good shepherd lays down his life for the sheep” (John 10:11).
26: Pekahiah’s summary. The record of another failed reign — evil perpetuated, killed by a subordinate — adds to the accumulating evidence that the northern monarchy is in terminal decline. The full picture of Israel’s kings in 2 Kings 15 is a portrait of systemic failure, which makes Christ’s kingship not an extension of this pattern but its radical cure.
27: Pekah reigns twenty years — by northern standards a long reign. His alliance with Rezin of Syria (the Syro-Ephraimite coalition of vv37, 2 Kings 16:5) will precipitate the crisis that generates Isaiah’s Immanuel prophecy (Isaiah 7:14). Christ is the Immanuel born in response to that crisis: the sign given to Ahaz is fulfilled not in Pekah’s lifetime but in the fullness of time (Galatians 4:4) when “the virgin shall conceive and bear a son and shall call his name Immanuel” (Isaiah 7:14; Matthew 1:23).
28: Pekah did not depart from Jeroboam’s sins. The penultimate northern king continues the cycle. The persistence of national sin through generations demonstrates that covenant obedience cannot be produced by human effort or royal example alone. The new covenant addresses this root: “I will put my law within them and write it on their hearts” (Jeremiah 31:33) — the internalization of Torah through the Spirit that Christ sends fulfills what no northern king could achieve externally.
29: Tiglath-Pileser takes Galilee and Naphtali. This is the historical event that Isaiah 9:1-2 addresses: “In the former time he brought into contempt the land of Zebulun and Naphtali, but in the latter time he has made it glorious, the way of the sea, beyond the Jordan, Galilee of the nations.” Matthew 4:15-16 quotes Isaiah 9:1-2 directly to frame the beginning of Jesus’s Galilean ministry: “The people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light.” The territory reduced to darkness by Tiglath-Pileser’s conquest in 2 Kings 15:29 becomes the specific region of the dawning light of Christ’s ministry.
30: Hoshea assassinates Pekah — the last northern king comes to power through the same violent means as his predecessors. The cycle of regicide ends only with the external force of Assyria ending the northern monarchy altogether. No king from within Israel could reform the system; the system had to end. Christ does not reform the old covenant system — he fulfills and supersedes it: “The law was given through Moses; grace and truth came through Jesus Christ” (John 1:17).
31: Pekah’s summary. Nine kings of Israel have been noted in 2 Kings 15; only one (Zechariah) ends the dynasty as promised. The rest are assassinated or die in obscurity. The political tragedy of 2 Kings 15 is also a spiritual tragedy: “They did not cry to me from the heart when they wailed upon their beds” (Hosea 7:14). What was missing — true repentance, genuine return — is what Christ produces through his Spirit in those who are his.
32: Jotham son of Uzziah becomes king of Judah. The parallelism of northern chaos and southern continuity throughout the chapter is striking: while six kings succeed each other violently in Samaria, Judah has one king through most of the same period. The Davidic dynasty’s greater stability is an anticipation of the eternal Davidic throne Christ occupies. Not every Judean king is good, but the line continues; not every northern king is evil, but the line collapses.
33: Jotham: 25 years old, reigns 16 years. The regnal formula locates him precisely in historical time. Christ’s incarnation is similarly time-precise: “But when the fullness of time had come, God sent forth his Son” (Galatians 4:4). The specificity of biblical chronology — kings, years, synchronisms — grounds redemptive history in real time. Christ does not enter myth but history, just as Jotham enters history with a specific age and reign-length.
34: Jotham did right as his father Uzziah had done — another qualified positive evaluation. The chain of partial faithfulness (Uzziah → Jotham, both right but with qualifications) shows how the best human kings can only partially fulfill the covenant ideal. Christ is the unqualified fulfillment: every positive evaluation of Israel’s kings is “right in the eyes of the LORD, except...”; Christ is right without exception, the only king who meets the Deuteronomic standard completely.
35: High places not removed — Jotham builds the upper gate of the temple. The combination of partial faithfulness and partial failure characterizes Jotham exactly as it does his father. But Christ builds a greater temple through a greater faithfulness: “I will build my church, and the gates of hell shall not prevail against it” (Matthew 16:18). Jotham’s upper gate is a building project; Christ’s church is a living structure of people “built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, Christ Jesus himself being the cornerstone” (Ephesians 2:20).
36: Jotham’s regnal summary. The accumulation of these summary notices — kings who reigned and passed — points forward to the one who does not simply reign and pass. Psalms 45:6: “Your throne, O God, is forever and ever” — addressed to the king, ultimately fulfilled only in Christ. The summary notices of 2 Kings 15 are the foil against which the eternal throne of Ps 45 stands out.
37: In those days YHWH begins to send Rezin and Pekah against Judah — the Syro-Ephraimite coalition that will threaten Ahaz and generate Isaiah’s Immanuel prophecy (Isaiah 7:1-17). The divine sovereignty over geopolitical events (“YHWH began to send”) frames the Assyrian threat as providential. Matthew’s Gospel repeatedly uses the same framing: “This took place to fulfill what was spoken by the prophet”. The crisis of 2 Kings 15:37 is the providential occasion for the greatest messianic prophecy of Isaiah’s book, fulfilled in Christ.
38: Jotham buried in the city of David; Ahaz succeeds him. The peaceful dynastic transfer — father to son without assassination, unusual by northern standards — continues in Judah. Ahaz will be the worst of Judah’s kings in this era, but the line continues. The Davidic dynasty persists through good kings and bad because the covenant with David is not based on the individual king’s merit: “For the sake of David my servant I will not tear away all the kingdom” (1 Kings 11:13). This same covenant-fidelity reaches its fullest expression in the one who is both son of David and the eternal Son of God.
